SVThe Session Viewer· a case study
Overview The problem Research Ideation & IA Testing Execution Retrospective
See the views
Product design case study · Second Brain's flagship surface · 2026

How I transformed the black box of autonomous AI work into a story any teammate can replay.

Agents now do real work unattended and in parallel, and the only record of what they did is a machine log no human can read. The result arrives, but the hours of decisions, wrong turns, and parallel work behind it stay locked away. The Session Viewer opens that black box: one session, five ways of seeing it, so anyone on the team can read, watch, and judge what an AI did in their name.

My role · Product Designer Timeline · Jun–Jul 2026 Part of Second Brain
Start with the black box Jump to the five views

▼worked · 11 min · 2 agents · 1 question2 AGENTS
READpayments/dashboard.tsx2s

CLAUDEExport shipped, includes archived rows, capped at 50k per file.
One turn = your message + the reply. Everything between lives in "worked", open only when you want it.
Overview & impactThe 60-second version

What this is, in a paragraph.

AI coding tools record everything they do, as raw machine logs nobody can read. The moment agents started running autonomously, that stopped being an annoyance and became a real problem: I was approving work I couldn't inspect. The Session Viewer turns a session's log into five human-readable projections, a transcript you can read, a story you can follow, two maps you can explore, and a timeline that shows where the time went. This case study covers the design; the engineering underneath it is a separate write-up.

Problem

AI agents now work for hours unattended, overnight, and in parallel. The only record of what they did is a raw machine log no human can realistically read. Drift, errors, and wasted effort stay invisible until too late; auditing a run means log archaeology.

Solution

One recording, five projections. A turn/"worked" model gives every session a human spine, a semantic vocabulary types each action so a run can be scanned without reading it, and each view answers exactly one named question. A replay control plays the session back like a recording. The design is agent-native by construction: agents are first-class objects, and every agent is itself a session you can open.

My role

Product designer: I designed the unit of work, the semantic vocabulary, and the five views, and proved them in code against real sessions; the data infrastructure underneath is engineering-team craft and its own story.

Impact
  • Audit time: from the better part of an hour in raw logs to minutes in the viewer (self-measured, n=1, stated as such).
  • Late discovery reversed: drift that once surfaced the next morning is what the views now expose by default; the working target, once tested beyond me, is drift caught before turn three.
  • Focus: 8 views at the peak, pruned to 5 that each answer a named question.
  • In daily use by me and my teammates to audit real agent runs, shipping in Second Brain's internal rollout.
The complex problemA black box you're accountable for

The work happens while you're not looking. That's the point, and the problem.

The promise of AI agents is that you can hand them real work and walk away. The runs in this project were briefed for exactly that: full autonomy, no approvals, run to completion. Autonomy was the requirement; my job as the designer was to make it inspectable.

Every run produced a result. But how it got there, whether the plan was followed, where it went wrong first, what it silently redid three times, lived in a machine log. A documented morning from the rollout period: I needed to check, so I read the log by hand, and what I found justified every fear:

me, auditing an agent run by reading raw logs · 2026-07-17 · verbatim, typos preserved. Quotes like this double as the project's real user research: the actual user I designed for, quoted from the working record

"i saw the logs it has not followed the instructions, it never called the storybook so it differed on the first step itself, so first verify that agents functioning"

The agent had gone off-script on step one, and the only reason anyone knew was that I happened to dig. Running several agents at once made it worse: no pulse, no progress, no way to even tell they were alive. Today this problem belongs to a small crowd, people running seriously autonomous, multi-agent AI work. I'm designing ahead of the market on a bet that this is what mainstream development looks like shortly; if that bet is wrong, this is a power tool for a niche. That is the gap the Session Viewer fills. Its mission, in the words I wrote when it clicked:

"think of it as a lense that you have created an harness and now you want to evaluate how it is working how you can improve that harness.", the viewer exists to make autonomous AI work readable, watchable, and judgeable.

See the baseline for yourself: the raw record vs the same moment, translated

This is the anti-dump thesis made visible. On the left, what the machine actually writes: one tool call costs two opaque records. On the right, the same moment after the viewer's one-time translation: one human fact.

The raw machine log · recreated lines, representative structure, dummy data
{"parentUuid":"b3f1a2c8-77d1-4e0a-9c2f-0d8f4e6a21b9","sessionId":"a7e2c4d1-5b90-4f3e-8a67-2c1d9e0b4f58","type":"assistant","timestamp":"2026-07-07T02:14:09.412Z","message":{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01Xk9vR2","name":"Read","input":{"file_path":"payments/dashboard.tsx"}}]},"requestId":"req_9f3c22"}

{"parentUuid":"e6a1c0d4-3b28-49f7-b5c1-8d2e7f0a4c63","sessionId":"a7e2c4d1-5b90-4f3e-8a67-2c1d9e0b4f58","type":"user","timestamp":"2026-07-07T02:14:11.037Z","toolUseResult":{"stdout":"…318 lines…"},"uuid":"c41a88e0-6f2d-4b9a-a3e5-1c7b0d9f2e84"}
The same moment in the viewer · one readable event inside a turn
YOU"add CSV export to the payments dashboard"
▼worked · 11 min
READpayments/dashboard.tsx2s

Two machine records, one human fact. The log is translated once into readable events; nobody downstream reads raw records again. "it is raw JSON... but user cannot understand anything from this."

The black box, itemized

Every one of these is a documented moment where the raw log failed me. Each view in the product exists to kill at least one of them.

01

Raw logs are unreadable to a human.

The default was a wall of machine text; the founding instruction was to never just dump it.

02

You can't tell what the agent did, or where it went wrong first.

"it never called the storybook so it differed on the first step itself." Finding that took manual log-archaeology that shouldn't have been necessary.

03

Parallel agents are invisible while they run.

"why are they very slow?", several agents working at once, and no way to see any of them.

04

You can read events, but you can't judge quality.

The record should let you assess a run, not just replay it. "this is the most important evaluation thing."

05

The unit of work was wrong.

Raw message counts made sessions look huge and meaningless; the fix was changing the unit itself.

06

Valuable signal was thrown away by default.

Hiding the gap where the AI's thinking used to be would be a small lie. The absence itself is information.

07

Sessions silently vanish.

Building the viewer surfaced its own dark truth, an entire project's history already deleted by the tool's retention: "there's nothing left for the app to show."

!

The stakes

Autonomy without observability isn't leverage, it's liability. If you can't inspect what an agent did, you either re-check everything by hand (losing the time you saved) or you ship work you never saw.

The constraints I designed within

Constraints are design material. Every screen in this case study traces back to at least one of these, and the execution section names the tie-backs explicitly.

Technical
  • Unreadable record. The only source of truth is a raw machine log: thousands of append-only events per session, in a legacy shape nothing upstream will change for us.
  • Scale of real sessions. Designed against my actual on-disk history, 141 real sessions, not curated demo data. The first version died against it.
  • Gaps in the record. Some of what happened (the AI's private thinking) is never stored. The record has holes, and the design must show them rather than paper over them.
  • Parallelism & recursion. Several agents can run inside one recording, and every agent is itself a full session. Sequential rendering lies about the work.
  • Vanishing history. The tool's own retention silently deletes sessions; one project had nothing left to show. Local-first data you don't capture is data you lose.
Business
  • Audit time is the product. The black box costs its owner the better part of an hour per audit. The business case is minutes, with drift caught while the run can still be corrected, so autonomy can scale safely.
  • One designer, end to end. No team to hand off to. I architected the models and constraints and executed inside them, iterating in code.
  • Weeks, not quarters. A Jun–Jul window, shipping into the same internal rollout as Second Brain.
  • Every view earns its tab. Each view is a permanent maintenance cost; a view that answers no named question gets cut.
Ethical
  • Owner-first trace. A session trace can read like surveillance. The trace belongs to the developer who ran it: a mirror for the owner, not a monitor for someone else.
  • Honest rendering. Absence is shown as absence, failures stay visible but quiet. Hiding a gap is a small lie about the session.
  • Verbatim record. The viewer translates, it never rewrites. "Mentioned" is never credited as "used", and nothing is summarized in a way the reader can't check.
Research & empathyThe working record is the user research

The users were documented before the product existed.

This project's user research is unusual and labeled as such: the verbatim working record. Every frustration below was typed by the actual user being designed for, in the moment the pain happened, typos preserved. It is a small sample with an honest bias, the researcher and the subject overlap, which is exactly why the archetypes that follow generalize the design targets beyond me.

the founding instruction · verbatim

"on the detail page we dont want to just dump the data… dumping everything will take a lot of load from the system."

redefining the unit of work · verbatim

"the messages number seems too high, we need to show the turns here. 1 turn = user's typed mesage and assistant response."

watching parallel agents run · verbatim

"why are they very slow? can you track their progress?"

when the interface hid unstored thinking · verbatim

"dont drop they are valuable insight"

The two people this has to serve

Two archetypes fall out of the record, and they pull the design in different directions. The viewer only works if it serves both without betraying either.

D

The Privacy-Wary Developer

owns the trace; ran the agents
What they want

To replay and audit their own runs: what did the agent do in my name, where did it drift first, was the brief followed. Full detail, nothing hidden, including the unflattering middle.

What they fear

That a tool which records every step of their work becomes a monitor. A session trace shows wrong turns, dead ends, and messy prompts; in the wrong hands that reads as a performance file.

What the design must do

Stay owner-first: the trace is a mirror for the person who ran it. Density is the reader's decision (the checkbox panel), rendering is honest but never judgmental, and failures render quiet, an error tag, not a red alarm.

L

The Reviewing Teammate / Lead

accountable for work they didn't brief
What they want

To audit an autonomous run without reading raw logs: a verdict in minutes. Was the plan followed? Where did the time and tokens go? Can I trust this enough to build on it?

What they fear

Signing off on work nobody actually saw, and the alternative being log archaeology: an hour of scrolling machine text to answer one question, so the check silently stops happening.

What the design must do

Make a session shareable as a story, keep evidence one click away (every claim opens the exact turn), and answer the named questions, what happened, what's the story, what's the shape, where did the time go, each in its own view.

The first real test of the design, stated in the working record and kept as this piece's bar: whether a developer who didn't brief a run can audit it without me in the room.

Ideation & IAThe structural thinking before any paint

Find the session's true shape, then project it.

A session recording is thousands of events. The design breakthrough wasn't a clever visualization, it was choosing the right unit so a human's mental model and the machine's record finally line up. Everything else in the product is a projection of that structure.

The unit · kills pain 01 & 05

One turn = your message + the reply. Everything between is "worked."

You asked, it answered, that's a turn. The dozens of steps in between collapse into a single "worked" strip you expand only when you care. A 400-event session becomes 12 readable turns.

"the turn means. first human message and for that human message last assitant response, is considered as the turn (whatever cmes inbetween counts as worked)"
The vocabulary · kills pain 02

Every kind of action gets its own identity.

Skills, external tools, commands, hooks, agents, and questions each look different, plus the mode the work ran in. You can scan a session and know what kind of thing happened without reading a word of it.

"we need to distinct them from the regular tool calls, just like we did for the agents and the questions"
The honesty rule · kills pain 06

Absence is a signal, show the gaps.

When the AI's private thinking isn't stored, the viewer still shows that it happened, and for how long. Hiding the gap would be a small lie about the session.

"dont drop they are valuable insight"
The recursion · kills pain 03

Every agent is a session you can open.

An agent spawned inside a session is itself a full session, openable, readable, judgeable, in every view. Turtles all the way down.

"if user clicks a redirect link for the agent it's session detail page appear's in the new tab where user can see it's session detail view just like normal sessions."
The density dial · kills pain 01

You choose how much you see.

A checkbox panel controls what appears on the timeline, agents, questions, tools, thinking. Density is the reader's decision, not the designer's guess: an auditor and a skimmer get different timelines from the same session.

"give one more feature where user can select which all, they want to see in the timeline, a checkbox or something"
The reference rule · kills pain 04

"Mentioned" is not "used."

The viewer only credits an artifact, a ticket, a doc, a story, if the AI actually acted on it, not merely if its name appeared in text. A small distinction that decides whether the evidence can be trusted.

"there is a difference between written somewhere and actually referring it. REferring it means it has taken some inspiration from that story or read that story."

The information architecture: from machine log to five answers

The whole system is one pipeline of meaning, drawn here in plain product language. Data flows down; each translation happens exactly once; the five views are leaves that never touch the raw record. That is what makes the design scale from a tiny session to an overnight multi-agent run without changing shape.

The raw machine log
One append-only stream, thousands of entries per session: messages, tool calls, agent forks, and results interleaved. The only source of truth, and unreadable by design.
▼translated once, never re-read raw
Readable, typed events
Each entry becomes one human-readable event carrying its kind from the semantic vocabulary: a tool, a skill, an agent, a question, an error, a gap. Every view downstream speaks this one language.
▼grouped into the human rhythm
Turns: the human spine
The unit defined above, now the spine every view shares.
YOU · "add CSV export to the payments dashboard"
worked · 14 events collapsed
CLAUDE · "Export shipped, capped at 50k per file."
one turn
▼projected into five views · one question each
CompleteThe reading view"What exactly happened?"
StoryThe narrative view"What's the story of this session?"
FlowThe shape view"What's the shape of the work?"
Story canvasStory, spatially"Can story and shape live together?"
WaterfallThe time view"Where did the time actually go?"

The "worked" strip is a state machine

The strip that holds the messy middle is the most load-bearing component in the product, so it was designed as a state machine, four states, each changing exactly three things: elevation, color, and density. The error state is the ethical one: a failed step is never allowed to hide inside a collapsed strip.

Default→ pointer enters →Hover→ click →Expanded→ click again →Default·Errorsurfaces in any state, expanded or not
01Default
▶worked · 11 min · 2 agents
  • Density: one quiet summary line: duration, agents, questions.
  • Color: muted foreground, no fill.
  • Elevation: flat, hairline border only. The middle earns no attention until you ask.
02Hover
▶worked · 11 min · 2 agents
  • Density: unchanged, nothing opens yet.
  • Color: text darkens to ink, caret takes the accent.
  • Elevation: lifts one step (shadow appears). The strip announces it can open, without opening.
03Expanded
▼worked · 11 min
READdashboard.tsx
AGENTapi-builder
thinking · not stored
  • Density: the full worked log, grouped by kind: tools, agents, questions, gaps.
  • Color: the vocabulary carries it; the container stays neutral.
  • Elevation: a dashed rule separates machinery from conversation.
04Error
▶worked · 11 min1 ERROR
  • Density: collapsed, but the failed step surfaces itself on the summary line.
  • Color: a quiet error tag, deliberately not an alarm: "no need to hilight the agent card in red... just the error tag will work."
  • Elevation: unchanged. Only the signal changes, never the volume.

The Complete view, deconstructed

The deconstruction is done on the shipped screen itself: six structural decisions, each marked where it lives on the real UI. The skeleton is annotated in place, so what carries each decision is never in doubt.

1 2 3 4 5 6
1

Session identity and the five view tabs live on one bar. One recording, many projections: switching tabs never changes the data, only the question being asked of it.

2

The timeline rail sits left, always visible. Constant orientation while you scroll, and it doubles as navigation: the rail is the map, the spine is the territory.

3

Turns are the spine. The page reads top to bottom in the human rhythm, you said, it answered, because that is the unit a reader can hold in their head.

4

The worked strip sits between, collapsed by default. The machinery is one click away, never zero: density is opt-in (the state machine above).

5

Detail lives in a side panel, not a new page. Opening a node slides the panel over this zone and never navigates away, so you never lose your place. This rule exists because Flow v1 broke it, and the autopsy is in the next section.

6

The density panel rides the rail. Checkboxes decide what the timeline shows, agents, questions, tools, thinking, so the reader chooses the altitude, not the designer.

Prototyping & usability testingThe failures are the spine

The prettiest thing in the product failed first.

None of this was designed on paper and built once. Each version chased one problem, failed somewhere honest, and passed what survived to the next. The founding failure set the tone: "v1 parsed on every view and literally crashed from it (the OOM bugs)", the first build died against real data, and every design decision after it was tested the same way, against my full on-disk history, not curated demos.

The Flow autopsy

Flow v1 was the product's showpiece: a full canvas of the session as a node graph. It demoed beautifully and failed its first real use. Here is its structure, stripped to gray boxes to show exactly why. (The shipped v1 was high-fidelity; this deconstruction removes the paint. One honesty note: the real canvas arranged itself by layout physics, so it was far messier than this; the geometry here is tidied just enough that each failure can be pointed at.)

1

No fixed origin. The eye has no guaranteed entry point; "session start" is just another box somewhere on a canvas.

2

Edges cross freely. Reading order is a guess, and every pan re-shuffles the mental map you were building.

3

Same kinds scatter. Agents, questions, and edits land wherever layout physics drop them, so spatial memory never forms: the two agents belong together and sit far apart, the dotted line is the relation the layout ignores.

The usability verdict came from real use, the day I opened it on a real session, and it is the most important quote in this case study:

Flow v1 · beautiful, disorienting

"the current 'flow' tab looks great. but when i open that, i loose the track. And we need to show it in a way that user actually comes and see this. and actually understands the fow of their session." A map that makes you feel lost has failed at being a map, no matter how good it looks.

The call · don't replace it, split the question

The insight was that Flow was being asked more than one question at once: what is the raw shape of execution? (which it answered well) and what's the story, spatially? (which it didn't). So: "keep the current flow tab as it is. we need to create a new flow tab...", and the split named one question per view: Flow kept the shape question, Story canvas took the story, and Waterfall's where did the time go? was named in the same pass.

Flow v2 · the Story canvas

The second flow view puts the story on the canvas: chapter cards you can pan through, with parallel work drawn as a real branch. The part I refused to compromise on, verbatim: "THing which i liked most is the parallal agent in the story is perfect how a user can see what is happening." And the cards had to have character. "We need the colored cards like previous one, the solid dark borders and light tint in the middle."

Drawn as the decision flow it actually was, the autopsy ends in a split, one reader question per view:

Flow v1: one canvas, asked every question at once"looks great. but when i open that, i loose the track"
▼which question is the reader actually asking?
"What is the raw shape of execution?"
Flow · kept as-is

The classic graph stays, it was answering this question well. Detail opens in a side panel so you keep your place.

"What happened, in what order?"
Story canvas · new

Chapters laid out spatially with parallel work as a real branch: narrative and shape in one picture, with a stable reading order.

"Where did the time go?"
Waterfall · new

Bars against the clock. Overlap proves parallelism was real; the longest gap turns out to be the AI waiting on a human.

The iteration story, version by version

The journey without theater: each version below chased one named problem and passed what survived forward. Two numbers describe the pruning better than any adjective: 8 views at the peak, 5 that earned their tab; and a design corpus of 141 real sessions, which is what kept the failures honest.

v1late JunStop dumping data; make a session readable. Invented the turn/"worked" model and the collapsible middle. Proved the concepts, then buckled under big real sessions and became unusable, forcing everything after.the vocabulary
research forkJul 1Study what a session even contains. A deliberate pause: map everything that happens between a question and its answer, so no event type would ever surprise the design again.the study
v2Jul 2Make it a story. The first fully autonomous build, run against a brief and guardrails I had written in advance. Story view and the flow canvas were born, and Flow v1's "i loose the track" failure with them.the narrative
v4Jul 4Make it a product (the record jumps from v2 to v4). The workhorse: light mode, eight views, the guided tour, relentless fit-and-finish ("do 10 iterations on this product"). Also where view-bloat peaked and pruning began.the polish
v5 ★Jul 6–7The reframe. "We are evolving our product for the age of AI harnesses", from log reader to evaluation surface. Rebuilt to stay fast on real sessions; time-travel playback designed; ran v4 and v5 side by side to prove the difference.the pivot
v6 → desktopJul 10+Ship it properly. Trimmed to the views that earned their place, made messy tool output readable, added export-a-session-for-LLM-grading, and moved to a desktop app, migrated by gated autonomous agents, reviewed at every step.shipping

A caveat the method deserves: the viewer was largely built by the agents it observes, and the design corpus was my own history, which risks biasing the views toward what agents produce rather than what a human auditor needs. The counterweight so far is my own daily auditing; the real correction will come from users who aren't me.

The views that lived, and the ones that died

The product didn't start with these five. At its peak the tab bar held eight views, and I killed the ones that couldn't answer a real question. The honest failure: tree-style graph views, which I kept trying to force onto sessions that are mostly linear:

me, giving up honestly · 2026-06-30

"snake view needs to be something we need to reconsider... currently how it looks, it makes no sense... and honestly i also dont know what we can do with this, please find ideas how we can improve this?"

CompleteStoryFlowStory canvasWaterfall TimelineAgentsFilesArtifactsInsights TreeSnake

Green = the five this case study covers (the desktop release deliberately shipped with a trimmed set, a view has to earn its tab). Plain = supporting views that survived in the full product. Struck = killed. Playback sits apart: it isn't another projection, it's every projection replayed against the clock. The strip spans the whole journey, not one moment's tab bar; at its peak the bar held eight.

The surface's lineage, shared with Second Brain

The viewer's early UI grew from the same design lineage as the Second Brain prototypes, the serif-and-cream digest era, before diverging into the dark console the harness work demanded. Real earlier prototypes, mock data:

Shared lineage · the digest eraThe daily-digest concept both products started from, session summaries as prose cards. Readable, but nothing here could answer "what did the agent actually do?"▶ open this iteration
Shared lineage · sessions as recordsSessions rendered as structured records with per-session correction signals, the first hint of the turn/"worked" spine that Complete view formalized.▶ open this iteration
Final · the v6-style consoleThe dark, terracotta-accented console this journey landed on (mock session shown): timeline rail, semantic vocabulary, agents as first-class objects.▶ open this iteration
The emotional core of the whole project is one bad morning, verbatim: "i genuinly dont know what you understood while doing this... you wasted my time and tokens." An autonomous run had gone sideways and I found out too late. The viewer exists so that sentence never has to be typed again, next time, the drift is visible by turn two.
Final executionOne session, five ways of seeing it

Different questions need different views of the same session.

"What exactly happened?" and "where did the time go?" are different questions; forcing one screen to answer both is how tools become unreadable. So the viewer projects one recording into distinct views, each built to answer one question well, and each traceable to a named constraint from the problem section. Four of the five views below are screenshots of the real product; every frame also opens the fully clickable mockup with mock data. Waterfall and Playback are shown as recreations, labeled as such, until production screenshots exist.

View 1 · Complete. "What exactly happened?"kills pains 01 · 05 · 06
the real product · complete view▶ open the clickable mockup
Complete, the reading view. This screen exists because of the Unreadable record and Honest rendering constraints. The turn spine keeps a human rhythm (you said, it answered); the "worked" strip holds the machinery, expanded only on demand. Notice what the vocabulary does: agents, questions, errors, and even unstored thinking are visible at a glance, and the error carries a plain tag, not a red alarm. I tried louder treatments and the record pushed back; the quiet error tag won.
View 2 · Story. "What's the story of this session?"kills pains 01 · 02
the real product · story view · detail panel open▶ open the clickable mockup
Story, the narrative view. This screen exists because of the Audit time is the product constraint: a reviewing teammate needs how-it-went in minutes, not 400 events. The Story view compresses a session into chapters with the turning points intact, including the stumble. It's the view that made sessions shareable: you can hand a teammate a story where you could never hand them a log.
View 3 · Flow. "What's the shape of the work?"kills pains 02 · 03
the real product · flow · classic graph▶ open the clickable mockup
Flow, the shape view. This screen exists because of the Parallelism & recursion constraint: parallel work finally looks parallel. The two agents fan out into their own lanes and merge back, a rule I set early: "if parrallet agents or tool calls are done then they must appear parrall not sequential." Click a node and its detail opens on the right, so you never lose your place. The first Flow taught a hard lesson, see the Flow autopsy in the testing section.
View 4 · Story canvas. "Can story and shape live together?"kills pains 01 · 03
the real product · flow · story canvas▶ open the clickable mockup
Story canvas, the second flow view. This screen exists because of the Every view earns its tab constraint, applied in reverse: Flow v1 failed a named question, so a new view earned its place by answering it. The story's chapters laid out spatially, with the parallel agents drawn as an actual branch, narrative and shape in one picture. Clicking any card opens its detail on the right, same rule as everywhere. It's the design answer to "i loose the track."
View 5 · Waterfall. "Where did the time actually go?"kills pains 03 · 04
recreation · waterfall (no production screenshot exists yet)▶ open the clickable mockup
Waterfall, the time view. This screen exists because of the Audit time is the product constraint: time is the auditor's first question, so it gets a dedicated answer. The same session as bars against the clock. Two things pop instantly that no other view shows: the agents genuinely overlapped (parallelism was real, not claimed), and the single longest human-visible delay was the AI waiting for a person to answer a question. That second one changed how I write prompts.
+1 · Playback. "Can I just watch it happen?"kills pains 02 · 04
recreation · replay mid-session (no production screenshot exists yet)▶ open the clickable mockup
Playback, the view that moves. This exists because of the Unreadable record constraint taken to its conclusion: the most honest rendering of a recording is to play it. A transport control replays the session as it happened: "messages type in, tools flash, agents fork, the Flow canvas follows the playhead." This is where the whole design philosophy got its sentence. "Reading a log is forensic; watching a session is narrative." It reuses every view above, driven by a clock instead of a scrollbar, which is why it cost little and demos best.

The semantic vocabulary, as a UI kit

The component system underneath every view. Each kind of action has one chip, one color pair, and one placement grammar, standardized so the vocabulary scales across five views without re-teaching the reader. Color encodes signal priority: the neutral chip is the high-frequency, low-signal default; saturated identities are reserved for the rarer kinds an auditor actually hunts for.

Component
What it marks
Color logic
READ BASH
Tool calls. The mechanical majority of any session: file reads, commands, edits.
Deliberately the quietest chip: neutral gray on the card tint. High frequency, low signal, it must recede.
SKILL
Skills. Packaged procedures the run invoked; a different kind of act than a raw tool call.
Purple pair. "we need to distinct them from the regular tool calls."
MCP
External connections. Work that crossed into another system.
Blue pair: the "elsewhere" family, matches the view chrome for Complete.
AGENT
Agents. First-class objects; every agent is itself an openable session, and its line renders as an inset block.
The loudest identity in the kit: agents are the thing this product exists to make visible.
ASKED YOU PLAN MODE
Questions & modes. The run pausing on a human, and the mode the work ran in.
Amber pair: attention without alarm. Questions carry their waited-duration.
ERROR
Failures. A failed step; also surfaces on the collapsed worked strip (state machine).
The red pair, kept quiet by rule.
payments/dashboard.tsx
Files & artifacts. Named in mono ink, credited only when actually acted on, "mentioned" is not "used."
No chip at all: files are objects, not events, so they read as plain identifiers.
thinking · not stored
Absence. Unstored thinking rendered as a ghost line with its duration.
No color, italic, muted: present enough to be honest, quiet enough to not pretend it's content.
Chip typography
mono · 9.5px · 700
tracking .05em · UPPERCASE
Chip geometry
radius 5px · padding 2px 7px
one fg + one tint per kind
Line rhythm
worked lines: mono 11.5px
7px vertical · 10px gaps
Row rhythm
turns: 13px/16px padding
hairline separators only

The accessibility exhibit: the vocabulary without color

The kit's rule is that color is reinforcement, never the only channel: every chip states its kind as text at 700 weight, and placement grammar carries meaning too (agents indent as blocks, questions carry a waited-duration, absence renders italic). Below is the working proof: the identical component cluster, rendered normally and through a real grayscale filter.

Full color
READSKILLMCPAGENTASKED YOUERRORPLAN MODE
▼worked · 11 min1 ERROR
READpayments/dashboard.tsx2s
AGENTapi-builder
thinking happened here · 42s · content not stored
ASKED YOU"include archived rows?"waited 4 min
The same components · grayscale filter applied
READSKILLMCPAGENTASKED YOUERRORPLAN MODE
▼worked · 11 min1 ERROR
READpayments/dashboard.tsx2s
AGENTapi-builder
thinking happened here · 42s · content not stored
ASKED YOU"include archived rows?"waited 4 min

What survives grayscale: the label names the kind on every chip; tonal value still separates tinted identities from the neutral tool default; the inset block still says "agent", the italic ghost still says "absence", and the error still rides the collapsed header. A grayscale reader loses decoration, not information.

Measured contrast, the exact hex pairs in this page's stylesheet

PairLight themeRatioWCAGDark themeRatioWCAG
Body copy on canvas#374151 / #ffffff10.3:1passes AAA#d4d4d8 / #0f0f0f13.0:1passes AAA
Skill chip#6d28d9 / #ede9fe6.0:1passes AA#a78bfa / #2316406.1:1passes AA
MCP chip#1d4ed8 / #dbeafe5.5:1passes AA#60a5fa / #0e22446.2:1passes AA
Agent chip#be185d / #fce7f35.1:1passes AA#f472b6 / #3a12256.1:1passes AA
Question chip#b45309 / #fef3c74.5:1passes AA#fbbf24 / #3328108.7:1passes AAA
Error chip#dc2626 / #fee2e23.9:1passes the 3:1 large-text bar; below the 4.5:1 normal-text bar#f87171 / #3a12126.0:1passes AA
Tool chip#6b7280 / #f5f5f54.4:1passes the 3:1 large-text bar; just below 4.5:1#a1a1aa / #1a1a1a6.8:1passes AA

Ratios computed with the WCAG 2.1 relative-luminance formula for the exact hex values in this page's stylesheet. Chip labels render at 9.5px/700, which WCAG counts as normal text, so the strict 4.5:1 bar is the one that applies. Two light-theme pairs sit below it, and by design they are the two kinds whose identity never rides on the tint: the tool chip is the deliberate quiet default whose identity is its label, and the error chip prints its name and additionally surfaces on the collapsed header. The grayscale panel above is the proof that both remain fully legible as text and fully identifiable without color.

What became possible

A black box became legible, and measurably faster to audit. Self-measured, n=1, stated as such: the overnight audit that used to eat most of an hour now takes minutes. The drift that once took manual log-archaeology to find ("it never called the storybook") is exactly what the views now surface by default.
Parallel work became visible as parallel work. Agent lanes in Flow, the branch in Story canvas, overlapping bars in Waterfall, three independent answers to "are my agents even alive?"
Silent data loss was surfaced. The viewer is what revealed that an entire project's sessions had already been deleted by retention, the discovery that hardened Second Brain's "capture it before it vanishes" thesis. (pain 07, answered)
It became teachable and shareable. A guided tour for new users, explainer artifacts for teammates, and a cleaned public build, the black box stopped being a private problem I understood alone.
RetrospectiveEach lesson anchored to a real moment in the working record

What this actually taught me.

Two kinds of lessons came out of this work: what it taught me about designing ethically for people whose work is being recorded, and what it taught me about architecting a system of views over a stream nobody can read.

What I learned about ethical design

1
The failure you find too late is the one that hurts"you wasted my time and tokens". I wrote that at the end of a run I could have corrected at its second turn, if I could have seen it drift. That's the real cost of the black box: not bad output, but late discovery, and late discovery is an ethical problem before it is a technical one, because someone is accountable for work they never saw. It reframed the whole product for me: the viewer isn't documentation of the past, it's an early-warning system for work still in flight. Observability is what turns autonomy from a risk you tolerate into leverage you scale.
2
Honesty is a rendering decision, not just a writing oneThe day the interface hid a gap where the AI's thinking used to be. A trace viewer makes a hundred small honesty calls: show the gap or paper over it, credit "mentioned" as "used" or not, render the failure as an alarm or a tag. The rules that survived, absence is a signal, quiet errors, verbatim record, are what keep an owner-first mirror from sliding into either flattery or surveillance. Trust in autonomy is really trust in observability, and observability is only worth trusting if it refuses to lie politely.

What I learned about system architecture

3
Find the true unit first; everything else is projection"the messages number seems too high, we need to show the turns here." The turn/"worked" model did more for readability than every visualization combined, because it made the machine's record and the human's mental model agree on what a session is. Once the unit was right and the vocabulary was attached exactly once, five views became cheap: each is a projection of the same structure, and the architecture scales from a two-turn session to an overnight multi-agent run without changing shape.
4
A view has to answer a question, or it's decorationI spent real days on tree and snake graphs before admitting, verbatim, "it makes no sense... i also dont know what we can do with this." Most sessions are nearly linear, the fancy graph was answering a question nobody asked. The fix wasn't better rendering; it was naming the actual questions (what happened? what's the story? what's the shape? where did the time go?) and building one honest view per question. Every surviving view maps to one; everything that didn't got killed.

The through-line

01

Find the true unit first, the turn/"worked" model did more for readability than every visualization combined.

02

One view per question. A screen asked to answer two questions answers neither.

03

Show the gaps, keep the failures visible, dial the alarms down, honesty is a rendering decision, not just a writing one.

Where it stands, honestly

The honest limits. The five core views ship as v1 of the Session Viewer, going into the same internal rollout as Second Brain. Evidence of value so far is my own team's daily use, n=1 territory, stated as such; the first real test is whether a developer who never briefed the run can audit it with me out of the room. The five core views are mature; the evaluation capabilities (export-for-grading, playback) are newer and less proven in daily use. And the viewer observes quality, the deeper question of scoring it belongs to Second Brain's intelligence work, told separately.
✎
What was mine, and what wasn't I architected the interaction models and defined the system constraints; the execution ran inside guardrails I designed, iterated in code by one person, and every build was audited in the viewer itself, the product proving its own thesis on its own construction. Each decision was a bridge between the engineering reality of the log and what a human auditor needs. The production data infrastructure underneath is engineering-team craft and its own (separate) story.

Where this sits in the trilogy

Second Brain's thesis is that the knowledge inside AI sessions is worth capturing and can be made trustworthy. The Session Viewer is that thesis at its most literal: the most invisible work a team has, autonomous agent runs, turned into something you can read in a minute, watch like a recording, and judge with evidence.

A note on the evidence The quotes in this case study, including the unflattering ones, are verbatim from the project's real working record, typos preserved. The four core view screens are real product screenshots; the Waterfall and Playback frames and every inline component demo are recreations with dummy data; the production tool is walked through live. The engineering that makes these views fast on real sessions is deliberately out of scope and will be its own technical write-up.
